coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of 0D/2D homostructure for enhanced hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jianfang
Zhu, Tianyu
Wang, Yan
Cui, Jiewu
Sun, Jian
Yan, Jian
Qin, Yongqiang
Shu, Xia
Zhang, Yong
Wu, Jingjie
Tiwary, Chandra Sekhar
Ajayan, Pulickel M.
Wu, Yucheng
coredata.dc:description: Efficient exfoliation of layered materials has attracted considerable attention in various applications due to their superior photoelectric, physical and chemical properties. Here, we report a universal, rapid approach to prepare the two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets and zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots (QDs) using a simple cryo-mediation liquid phase exfoliation of layered materials (graphite, MoS2, WS2). The QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructure can be subsequently formed by the self-assembly of the as-exfoliated QDs and nanosheets. The unique structural properties of the mono- or few-layer mesoporous nanosheets interspersed with QDs can expose abundant active edge sites as well as improve the conductivity, which exhibits excellent activity and stability towards electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). This work offers a powerful methodology to prepare 2D homostructures from a variety of layered materials.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the nanosheets and QDs
Preparation of the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of GQDs decorated graphene by cryo-mediation liquid phase exfoliation approach.

TEM, HRTEM, AFM images and corresponding height of graphene (a, d, g, j), MoS2 nanosheets (b, e, h, k), WS2 nanosheets (e, f, i, l) prepared by cryo-mediation liquid phase exfoliation. The inset of (d–f) shows the SAED patterns of graphene, MoS2 nanosheets and WS2 nanosheets.

TEM images with corresponding size distribution and AFM images with corresponding height of the QDs: graphene (a, d, g, j), MoS2 (b, e, h, k), WS2 (e, f, i, l) prepared by cryo-mediation liquid phase exfoliation. The inset of (a–c) shows the HRTEM images of GQDs, MoS2 QDs and WS2 QDs.
TEM, HRTEM images and structure schematic of the QDs decorated nanosheets: (a, d, g) CL-graphene, (b, e, h) CL-MoS2 and (c, f, i) CL-WS2.

HER activities of the QDs decorated nanosheets: (a) polarization curves, (b) Tafel plots, (c) C dl, (d) Nyquist plots recorded at overpotential of 200 mV, (e) polarization curves initially and after 2000 CV sweeps at 100 mV s−1, and (f) time-dependent current density curves measured at an overpotential of 150 mV.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of 0D/2D homostructure for enhanced hydrogen evolution

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: J. F. Zhang and T. Y. Zhu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient exfoliation of layered materials has attracted considerable attention in various applications due to their superior photoelectric, physical and chemical properties. Here, we report a universal, rapid approach to prepare the two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets and zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots (QDs) using a simple cryo-mediation liquid phase exfoliation of layered materials (graphite, MoS2, WS2). The QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructure can be subsequently formed by the self-assembly of the as-exfoliated QDs and nanosheets. The unique structural properties of the mono- or few-layer mesoporous nanosheets interspersed with QDs can expose abundant active edge sites as well as improve the conductivity, which exhibits excellent activity and stability towards electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). This work offers a powerful methodology to prepare 2D homostructures from a variety of layered materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The atomically thin two-dimensional (2D) materials consisting of graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have been demonstrated to have a wide range of applications with superior performances, such as electronics, sensing, catalysis, and energy storage [1–5]. High active surfaces, easily tunability and different phases make these as attractive materials for catalysis [6,7]. In the catalysis areas, the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is regarded as a simple and environmentally friendly methodology to produce high-purity H2 [8–14]. In the past decade, the atomically thin graphene, MoS2 and WS2 materials have received wide attention in electrocatalytic HER, which are supposed to replace the platinum-based catalysts [15–18]. Up to now, much effort has been made for these materials to increase the efficiency and lower the overpotential of electrocatalytic HER.

Previous works have demonstrated that the key factors to improve the HER catalytic activity of the MoS2 and WS2 lie in the transformation of crystalline phase from 2H to 1T, expanding interlayer spacing for easy reactant access, decreasing thickness for efficient electron transfer, and especially increasing edge site density [19–25]. The zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots (QDs) of these materials consisting of more edges which are chemically active, shows multifold enhancement as compared to their 2D counterpart [26–28]. Benson et al. demonstrated that the MoS2 QDs show excellent electrocatalytic HER activity due to a large number of exposed active edges and the low number of MoS2 layers [29]. Ou et al. found that the defective MoS2 QDs with large surface area, abundance of the active site, and high conductivity exhibit high activity and stability towards the electrocatalytic HER [30]. Zhao et al. prepared the WS2 QDs by liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) process and achieved high HER activity with a onset potential of 90 mV and a Tafel slope of 51 mV dec−1 [31]. Many attempts have been done to prepare the QDs materials with abundant edge sites for electrocatalytic HER. However, exploring a large-scale and cost-effective strategy to synthesize stable and high efficient QDs materials is still a tremendous challenge.

The QDs materials can be easily reunited together either in organic solvents or in water due to the large surface energy, which leads to the restriction of exposing active sites and stability. In order to maximize the usage of active sites and improve the stability of the QDs materials, coupling of the QDs with few-layer materials to construct the 0D/2D heterostructures has been reported as an efficient route to increase the activity and stability of the catalysts [32–34]. For example, Najafi et al. employed the graphene flake as the scaffolds to disperse 2H-MoS2 QDs. Benefiting from the conductivity graphene scaffolds and abundant catalytic active sites of 2H-MoS2 QDs, these heterostructures exhibited high electrocatalytic HER performance [35]. Du et al. constructed a heterostructure of MoS2 QDs/VS2 nanosheets by a hydrothermal method. The possible synergistic effect of MoS2 QDs and metallic VS2 nanosheets led to the enhanced performance of electrocatalytic HER in acidic solution [36]. Apart from the 0D/2D heterostructure, the other heterostructures of 2D/2D and 0D/0D also have been investigated as electrocatalysts for HER [37,38]. Although the 0D/2D heterostructure can provide numerous catalytic and conductive active sites thus enhance the HER activity, the synthesis process often suffers from some limitations of a complicated process, small scale, and low yield. Compare to the heterostructure, the 0D/2D homostructure constructing from a single material also exhibits the high performance of electrocatalytic HER. In recent years, the ultrasonic liquid phase exfoliation (LPE) method has been successfully employed to synthesize MoS2 QDs/nanoflakes and the resultant products achieve excellent performance towards electrocatalytic HER [39,40]. The low-cost and straightforward LPE method is a very promising technique to produce the 0D/2D homostructure, however, it always confronts the issues of the low-yield of QDs and the thickness of nanosheets. Therefore, it is very meaningful and valuable to explore a facile, modified LPE method for building the 0D/2D homostructure.

Herein, we developed a simple and rapid cryo-mediation LPE strategy for the direct synthesis of the QDs decorated mesoporous nanosheets 0D/2D homostructure catalysts with abundant edge sites relative to basal plane sites, leading to enhanced electrocatalytic activity towards HER. Our procedure for the first exfoliation of QDs and the ultrathin nanosheets involves the following steps: an initial soaking of room-temperature layered materials powders into liquid nitrogen (quenching step) and then dispersion of soaked powders into a room-temperature solvent (recovering step). This procedure results in a dramatic temperature change of layered materials powders in a cycle, from room temperature to liquid nitrogen temperature and then returning to room temperature again. The thermal expansion in the recovery step helps weaken the interlayer force (Van der Waals force) of layered materials while the fast thermal relaxation in the quench step would form small cracks in the layered materials powders allowing solvent permeation, both of which facilitate the following liquid phase exfoliation as evidenced by the formation of few- and even mono-layer QDs and ultrathin mesoporous nanosheets [41,42]. The coexistence of the QDs and nanosheets in the solvent can be easily constructed into QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures via self-assembly after settling down over a period of time. The thicknesses of our synthesized QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures are found to be 1–3 nm, corresponding to 1–3 layers. The obtained 0D/2D homostructures exhibit superior electrocatalytic HER performance, especially for MoS2 0D/2D homostructure, which only needs a low overpotential of 86 mV to afford a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with a small Tafel slope of 35 mV dec−1. This work provides a new insight into the synthesis of the high electrocatalyst based on the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures from layered materials.

Graphite (99.99%), MoS2 (99%) and WS2 (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Isopropanol (IPA) and H2SO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 20 wt% Pt/C was obtained from Shanghai Hesen Electric Co., Ltd. The deionized water was produced by Direct-Q3 Water Purification Systems (KTT-111-10, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nanosheets and QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene, MoS2 and WS2 nanosheets and QDs were synthesized by cryo-mediated liquid phase exfoliation method with two steps. First, the bulk powder materials were immersed in liquid nitrogen for 6 h. Second, the cryo-pretreatment powders were dissolved in the IPA and DI water (volume ratio of IPA to H2O is 1:1) mixed solvent and then liquid phase exfoliated under ultrasonication for 2 h. The concentration of the dispersions was 3 mg mL−1 for the powders. The nanosheets and QDs were obtained by centrifugalization of the dispersions at 4000 rpm (rounds per minute) for 10 min. The bulk powder materials were also treated only by liquid phase exfoliation under the same condition as the cryo-mediated liquid phase exfoliation to explore the effect of cryo-pretreatment on the producing procedure and morphology of the nanosheets and QDs. In order to investigate the time-dependence influence on the cryo-mediated liquid phase exfoliation for the synthesis of mono- or few-layer nanosheets, an additional ultrasonication time from 30 min to 5 h was employed for the exfoliation of graphite as an example.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared dispersions were positioned in a glass beaker for overnight and the QDs were interspersed into the nanosheets by self-assembly method based on electrostatic interactions. The QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures were obtained in centrifugate at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures solution was coated on glassy carbon electrode for hydrogen evolution reaction.

The morphologies of as-prepared samples were studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F). The thicknesses of as-prepared samples were measured by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8.0, Bruker Nanoscope V). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Japan) was carried out to check the crystalline structures of the as-prepared samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250, American) was used to analyze the chemical state of the as-prepared samples. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.

All the electrochemical measurements were performed on CHI760E electrochemical workstation. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and graphite rod were worked as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The as-prepared dispersion was loaded onto a glassy carbon electrode (diameter of 5 mm, loading mass of ∼0.5 mg cm−2) acted as a working electrode by a dip-coating method. N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 was used as electrolytes. All of the potentials were calibrated to the RHE by using the equation of E(RHE) = E(SCE) + 0.0591pH + 0.2415 V. The electrocatalytic activities of these samples were evaluated by polarization curves using Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and the polarization curves were corrected for iR drop. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out at overpotential of 200 mV in a frequency range of 100 kHz and 0.1 Hz. To evaluate the electrochemically active surface areas of the catalysts, CV curves were collected with different scan rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1 in the potential window between 0.1 and 0.2 vs. RHE. The durability of catalysts was evaluated by performing CV method at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 for 2000 cycles in the potential between −0.5 and 0.1 V vs. RHE. The chronoamperometry tests were also recorded for 10 h to examine the durability.

Our synthesis process starts with cryo-pretreatment of layer materials (graphite, MoS2, WS2) in liquid nitrogen followed by bath ultrasonication assisted LPE in a mixture solvent of isopropanol (IPA) and H2O. The liquid nitrogen pretreatment of the raw powders will affect the mechanical properties, especially the intralayer molecular bond embrittlement induced by ultralow temperature treatment, which subsequently promotes the fracture of few-/mono-layer nanosheets into QDs [43]. Taking graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated graphene as an example, the bulk graphite powders were firstly immersed in liquid nitrogen for several hours, and then the cryo-pretreated graphite powders were dissolved into the IPA/H2O mixture solvent followed by bath ultrasonication LPE for 2 h. Finally, the highly dispersed suspension of GQDs decorated graphene 0D/2D homostructure was obtained by self-assembly from the resulting solvent standing for some time, as schematically demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out to characterize the morphological structure of the as-prepared samples, which were prepared by soaking in liquid nitrogen for 4 h and ultrasonication for 2 h. Fig. 2 a-c reveal the presence of the graphene, MoS2 and WS2 nanosheets with the lateral size of 3–6 μm, which are reasonable in accordance with the average particle size of the precursor powders (∼6 μm, see SEM in Fig. S1). The nanosheets are predominant single- or double-layer sheets. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the graphene, MoS2 and WS2 nanosheets confirm their single-crystalline structure with the measured lattice distance of 0.21, 0.27 and 0.27 nm (Fig. 2d-f), which are assignable to the (1 0 0), (1 0 0) and (1 0 0) faces of the hexagonal graphene, MoS2 and WS2, respectively. Accordingly, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns display one set of diffraction spots with a hexagonal pattern characteristic of graphene, MoS2 and WS2 single-nanosheet, respectively. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) was tested to measure the thickness of the three nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 2g–l. The average thicknesses of 1.2, 1.3, and 1.9 nm are observed for the graphene, MoS2 and WS2, respectively, further revealing their single- or few-layer structures. More interestingly, some mesoporous are also observed from the AFM images of the nanosheets, which are the origin of the formation of quantum dots. We further investigated the influence of ultrasonication time on the formation of nanosheets. Taking graphene nanosheets as an example, only by ultrasonication for 30 min, the bulk graphite powders can be easily exfoliated to multi-layer nanosheets because that the van der Waals force between 2D interlayer is weakened after soaking them in liquid nitrogen (Fig. S2a). When extending the ultrasonication time, the thickness of the graphene nanosheets decreases. In addition, the nanosheets are further disintegrated into single- or few-layer nanosheets with a small size or even into QDs (Fig. S2b–f). It is worthy to note that the QDs can be also generated by ultrasonication time less than 2 h, but the amount of the QDs increases with prolonging of ultrasonication time, as indirectly evidenced by the presence of increased mesopore density on basal planes of nanosheets (Fig. 2g–i). The QDs were also characterized by TEM and AFM. TEM images show the shape of QDs is nearly circular with a relatively narrow diameter distribution (Fig. 3 a–c). The inserted HRTEM images clearly reveal that the QDs are well crystallized with lattice distance of 0.21, 0.27 and 0.27 nm observed for the GQDs, MoS2 QDs and WS2 QDs, respectively, which is consistent with the results of the nanosheets. The average sizes of 1.6, 1.8 and 2.2 nm are observed for the GQDs, MoS2 QDs and WS2 QDs, respectively (Fig. 3d–f). The thicknesses of the QDs are found to be from 0.4 to 2.5 nm measured by the AFM, which corresponds to 1–3 layers (Fig. 3g–l). Moreover, in order to illustrate the influence of liquid nitrogen pretreatment on the LPE process, these three nanosheets were prepared only by bath ultrasonication LPE for the same time without soaking in liquid nitrogen. Although some nanosheets can be obtained after ultrasonication for 2 h (Fig. S3), it is clearly seen that the thicknesses of these three nanosheets are much thicker than those prepared by cryo-mediation LPE technique (Fig. S4). The above results demonstrate that the cryo-mediation LPE technique is a simple and effective approach to prepare 2D single- or few-layer nanosheets and QDs.

Because of the coexistence of the nanosheets and QDs in the solvent, the QDs are easily interspersed into the nanosheets via self-assembly after settling down over a period of time. Fig. 4 a shows the TEM image of the highly dispersed GQDs decorated on graphene (denoted as CL–graphene). The corresponding HRTEM image reveals the GQDs intersperse into both the edge and internal surface of the single-layer graphene (Fig. 4d). The same results can be observed from the TEM and HRTEM images of MoS2 QDs decorated MoS2 nanosheets (CL-MoS2) and WS2 QDs decorated WS2 nanosheets (CL-WS2) (Fig. 4b, c and Fig. 4e, f). The corresponding structural schematic diagrams of the CL-graphene, CL-MoS2, and CL-WS2 are presented in Fig. 4g, h, and i, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were used to study the crystalline structures of CL-graphene, CL-MoS2 and CL-WS2 (Fig. S5). Compared to the patterns of bulk powders and the nanosheets prepared only by LPE (L-graphene, L-MoS2 and L-WS2), only a diffraction peak at 2θ = 14.4° corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane can be seen in the XRD patterns of these as-prepared samples, indicating the random restacking of highly exfoliated nanosheets [3]. The (0 0 2) peak is assigned to the interplanar diffraction. The (0 0 2) peak is broadened suggesting the reduced thickness of nanosheets. The chemical compositions of as-prepared samples were explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS results demonstrate that all these samples prepared by LPE with or without cryo-pretreatment are not been oxidized or functionalized but in the pristine chemical state (Fig. S6).

To further study the characteristic of the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures, the as-prepared samples were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectra. For example, both the CL-graphene and L-graphene display one adsorption peak at about 228 nm and another peak at 286 nm compared to bulk graphite which has no obvious adsorption peak (Fig. S7a). The peak at 228 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains whereas the peak at 286 nm can be ascribed to the n–π* transition of a small fraction of CO groups [44]. The increasing peak signal of CL-graphene at 228 nm can be ascribed to the existence of GQDs, indicating the formation of GQDs decorated graphene 0D/2D homostructure. The same results can be observed from MoS2 and WS2. Both CL-MoS2 and L-MoS2 show four characteristic peaks at 395, 450, 610 and 660 nm (Fig. S7b), respectively. The peaks at 395 and 450 nm are attributed to the direct transition from the deep valence band to the conduction band, and the peaks at 610 and 660 nm are ascribed to the K point of the Brillouin zone [45,46]. Noticeably, on the spectra of CL-MoS2, the signal of these four characteristic peaks decrease, while, a peak signal in the near-UV region (below 300 nm) increases, illustrating the thicknesses of MoS2 nanosheets become thin and MoS2 QDs have formed in the solution. The UV–vis spectra of WS2 reveal a similar tendency towards the absorption peaks (Fig. S7c). In contrast, the bulk MoS2 and WS2 only show the peaks of the indirect band gap in UV–vis spectra due to their indirect band gap characteristic of the bulk material [47]. All the above results confirm that the 0D/2D homostructures of QDs decorated nanosheets are successfully fabricated by cryo-mediation LPE technique.

The presence of abundant active edge sites in the 0D/2D homostructure makes it a promising electrocatalyst for HER due to involving of both QDs and mesoporous nanosheets. The HER activities of these materials were evaluated in 0.5 M H2SO4 using a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell. As a comparison, the bulk powders were tested and showed very weak HER activities (Fig. S8a). Their activities are greatly enhanced with conventional liquid phase exfoliation into nanosheets (Fig. S9a). Remarkably, the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures exhibit tremendously improved HER activities compared to nanosheets and QDs prepared by cryo-mediation LPE (Fig. 5 a and Fig. S10a, c). The comparison of performance towards HER among these materials is listed in Table S1. The CL-graphene, CL-MoS2 and CL-WS2 show small onset potential of 94, 67 and 78 mV, respectively. They require overpotential of 125, 86 and 92 mV to afford a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and of only 278, 183 and 218 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, respectively. These values are much lower than those of the bulk powders, LPE nanosheets and cryo-mediation LPE nanosheets and QDs. The corresponding Tafel slopes are only about 87, 35 and 40 mV dec−1 for the CL-graphene, CL-MoS2 and CL-WS2, respectively (Fig. 5b), which are much smaller than those of the bulk powders (Fig. S8b), LPE nanosheets (Fig. S9b) and cryo-mediation LPE nanosheets (Fig. S10b) and QDs (Fig. S10d). The CL-MoS2 and CL-WS2 show higher activity than most of the other molybdenum or tungsten sulfide as reported before (Table S2)

The enhanced electrocatalytic activity towards HER of the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures can be attributed to two factors: (1) the QDs and the mesoporous nanosheets possess a unique defect-rich structure and a very high electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), which provides more active edge sites for HER [48]. The ECSAs of these samples are measured by the double layer capacitance (Cdl) using cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 5c, the calculated Cdl of the CL-graphene, CL-MoS2 and CL-WS2 are 11.4, 20.7 and 15.5 mF cm−2, respectively, which are much higher than those of the bulk powders (Fig. S8c) and nanosheets (Fig. S9c), indicating a much larger ECSA and more active edge sites of the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures. (2) the reduced thickness of mono- or few-layer nanosheets eliminates interlayer potential barrier in the vertical direction along with the layers so that minimizes the resistivity perpendicular to the layer basal planes and thus improves the efficiency of electron transfer in the basal plane [27,49], as demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The Nyquist plots reveal much lower charge-transfer resistance for the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures (Fig. 5d), reflecting from the diameter of the semicircle, compared to the bulk powders (Fig. S8d) and nanosheets (Fig. S9d). Benefiting from the QDs decorated nanosheets homostructures, the synergistic effect of QDs with more active edge sites and mono- or few-layer nanosheets with accelerating the electron transfer rates improves the electrocatalytic activity for the HER.

The operational stability is also an important criterion for HER electrocatalyst. Fig. 5e shows the comparison polarization curves for the three QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures in the initial and after 2000 cycles. There are almost negligible changes in the polarization curves, illustrating the good catalytic stability for these samples. Moreover, the long-term stability of these three samples was investigated by i-t curves as illustrated in Fig. 5f. The current densities remain unchangeable under the overpotential of 200 mV for at least 10 h, revealing good electrocatalytic stability upon the long-term test.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple cryo-mediation LPE technique to fabricate the 0D QDs and 2D nanosheets. Benefiting from the cryogenic pretreatment in liquid nitrogen, the mono- or few-layer nanosheets, as well as the QDs, can be easily produced within a short time compared to nanosheets from conventional LPE process alone. More interestingly, these exfoliated QDs can be spontaneously interspersed onto the 2D nanosheets to construct the QDs decorated nanosheets 0D/2D homostructures, which exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activities and stabilities towards HER. For example, the MoS2 QDs decorated nanosheets (CL-MoS2) 0D/2D homostructure shows a low overpotential of 86 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 35 mV dec−1 with good stability. This cryo-mediation LPE technique provides a universal approach to devise and fabricate the desirable 0D/2D homostructured nanomaterials for promising applications in catalysis, energy storage, biomedicine and so on.

We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Self-assembly of 0D/2D homostructure for enhanced hydrogen evolution”.

Jianfang Zhang: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Tianyu Zhu: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Yan Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Jiewu Cui: Methodology, Resources. Jian Sun: Formal analysis, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jian Yan: Investigation, Resources. Yongqiang Qin: Data curation, Investigation. Xia Shu: Methodology, Software. Yong Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Jingjie Wu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Chandra Sekhar Tiwary: Methodology, Software, Writing - review & editing. Pulickel M. Ajayan: Supervision. Yucheng Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (51772072, 51672065, 51701057, U1810204), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province, China (1708085ME100), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (PA2019GDQT0022, PA2019GDQT0015, JZ2019HGBZ0142). We would like to thank the financial support from the 111 Project, China “New Materials and Technology for Clean Energy” (B18018). Dr. Y. Wang and Dr. J. W. Cui would also like to thank the financial support from the China Scholarship Council, China during their visits to Prof. Pulickel M Ajayan’s group at Rice University for collaborative research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mattod.2020.02.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
